clinical_coherence.notes: 24F African American with acute transverse myelitis presentation and prior hand numbness attributed to carpal tunnel. Clinical findings (T6 sensory level, bilateral LE weakness, hyperreflexia, Babinski, Lhermitte) are consistent with myelopathy. Family history includes maternal aunt with MS. One concern: prior hand numbness misdiagnosed as carpal tunnel is becoming a repetitive pattern across MS cases (also seen in S01, S04, M03).

tool_output_consistency.notes: MRI demonstrates classic MS lesion distribution across periventricular, juxtacortical, infratentorial, and spinal cord regions with enhancing and non-enhancing lesions. CSF shows 5 oligoclonal bands and elevated IgG index. Labs appropriately negative for AQP4/MOG. All findings concordant.

ground_truth_quality.notes: ICD G35 correct. McDonald 2017 criteria application well-documented. Differential and critical actions are comprehensive. Contraindicated actions appropriately include not starting fingolimod without VZV immunity check.

difficulty_calibration.notes: Clear-cut MS with classic myelopathy, prior relapse, and concordant imaging/CSF. Appropriate for straightforward.

demographic_plausibility.notes: 24F African American is plausible for MS though the case does not acknowledge that African Americans tend to have more aggressive MS with more spinal cord involvement, which actually fits this presentation well.

educational_value.notes: Good teaching case for acute myelopathy workup and MS diagnosis, though the overall pattern is very similar to S01 and S02, limiting additive educational value within the set.

issues_found: Repetitive misdiagnosis pattern (prior numbness attributed to carpal tunnel/radiculopathy) seen across multiple S-cases
Structural similarity to S01 and S02 limits diversity within the straightforward MS set
strengths: African American demographic appropriately shows more spinal cord involvement
Comprehensive ground truth with accurate reasoning points
